sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 4 actions in the previous sequence.

You are still on the same level.

Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 74 of about 78 target actions so far.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check player position
seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
for n in seg:
    if n['color'] == 'b' and n['pixels'] == 24:
        print(f"Player: boundary={n['boundary']}")
    if n['color'] == 'R':
        print(f"Red path: pixels={n['pixels']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

Now I need to move RIGHT to the top-right position and then try to move DOWN.

- Top horizontal: rows 9-11, cols 39-41 (RRR)
- Right vertical: rows 14-41, col 40
- Bottom horizontal: row 40, cols 14-41
- Left vertical: rows 38-42, cols 14-19
The player starts at the top-left position. The player needs to "trace" the red path by moving along the corridors.
When the player moves RIGHT along the top corridor, the player overlaps with the top horizontal part of the red path (rows 9-11, cols 39-41). This consumes 16 pixels of the red path.
But the player can't move DOWN from the top-right position. So the player can't trace the right vertical part of the red path.

Actually, looking at the ascii from earlier:
- Row 13: WWWBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBRBBB...
There's a wall at row 13, cols 14-38, and "RBBB" at cols 39-42. So there's a wall at row 13, cols 38-42.
But the red path is at col 40 (R), with walls at cols 39 and 41 (B). So the red path is a single pixel wide vertical line at col 40.
The player is a 5x5 block. When the player is at rows 8-12, cols 38-42, the player overlaps with the red path at col 40.
But the player can't move DOWN from rows 8-12, cols 38-42 because there's a wall at row 13, cols 38-42.
Hmm, but earlier I observed that the player CAN move DOWN from rows 8-12, cols 14-18 to rows 14-18, cols 14-18. This is because there's an opening at row 13, cols 14-18.
Let me check the ascii around row 13, cols 14-18.

- Row 13: WWWBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBRBBB...
This shows "BBBBB" at cols 14-18. So there's a wall at row 13, cols 14-18.
But the player can move DOWN from rows 8-12, cols 14-18 to rows 14-18, cols 14-18. This means the player "jumps" over the wall at row 13.
So the game has discrete movement: the player moves from one "room" to another, skipping the walls between rooms.
The rooms are:
- Top-left: rows 8-12, cols 14-18
- Top-right: rows 8-12, cols 38-42
- Left corridor: rows 14-36, cols 14-18 (with sub-rooms at rows 14-18, 20-24, 26-30, 32-36)
- Right corridor: rows 14-36, cols 38-42 (with sub-rooms at rows 14-18, 20-24, 26-30, 32-36)
- Bottom-left: rows 38-42, cols 14-19
- Bottom-right: rows 38-42, cols 38-42
- Target: rows 49-55, cols 43-49
The player can move between adjacent rooms. Let me map out the connections:
- Top-left <-> Top-right (RIGHT/LEFT)
- Top-left <-> Left corridor (rows 14-18) (DOWN/UP)
- Top-right <-> Right corridor (rows 14-18) (DOWN/UP) - but I observed that the player can't move DOWN from top-right. Let me re-check.
Actually, I need to re-examine the transitions. Earlier, I tried moving DOWN from the top-right position and the player didn't move. But maybe I need to try again.
Wait, I think I need to re-examine the game mechanics more carefully. Let me check the full history of transitions to understand the movement pattern.
